text:
🧑‍🎨 Becoming An AI Designer
A set of resources for upskilling from one of the 'traditional' digital design roles (UI, UX, Service) towards becoming AI aware, and on to be an AI designer.
🎯 Start Here: Begin Your Journey
If you're new to AI in design try this path:
Mindset Change
- Read Buzz Usborne's advice on approaching AI as infrastructure, not outcome
Get to grips with fundamentals
- Crash course for total beginners
First Steps
- Explore key beginner topics below
Then explore topics as you need them. This resource is modular. Dip into specific sections as your needs evolve.

🏁 Getting Started With AI
First Steps
For beginners: Start with
Mindset Change
, then
Fundamentals
, then explore
Thinking
resources,
Prompt writing
basics, and the
Prompting
guide.
Mindset Change
Advice from
Buzz Usborne
for any designer getting started
Tool, not an outcome.
Consider AI as infrastructure (like electricity: invisible, powerful, and everywhere). AI is not the work, it's what powers it.
AI gives you first drafts. Craftsmanship is what happens next.
AI won’t replace designers. But designers who use AI well might replace those who don’t.
Be curious, play, break things, try the hot new tool. Lean into discomfort. Explore why you dislike or love AI.
Lean on qualities that AI does exceptionally well (like unbiased thinking and data interpretation) and use those to compliment (not replace) your skills.
Consider AI as a thought-partner; use it to challenge assumptions, make ideas more robust and pull you from dead-ends.
Give AI a face; An art director, a researcher, a concept artist. Never you.
Be aware that using AI atrophies one’s ability to think critically, but improves cognitive bias. AI can make you more accurate, but less thoughtful.
Make time to dwell in the unknown, to write and re-write, sketch and be confused. Maintain space to understand a problem more deeply.
AI cannot produce the unexpected, illogical, magical or delightful. These are the parts that humans do well, and look for in great work.
"Writing is the process by which you realize that you do not understand what you are talking about. Importantly, writing is also the process by which you figure it out.”
AI presents an opportunity for unexpected creativity. If you consider AI as a turbo-charge to get to 80% completion, then it puts a much higher burden on (and opportunity for) designers to go above and beyond for weird and creative execution.
Get to grips with the fundamentals
Crash course for total beginners and a power-up for enthusiasts
https://2weeks.ai/
A Visual Introduction To AI For Designers, by Berkeley University
https://www.uxai.design
- Introduces AI and Explainable AI (XAI) concepts specifically for UX designers, with tools that support human-centered design for AI products and services
Thinking
The emerging role of the AI designer
https://www.matiasvaara.com/insights/the-emerging-role-of-the-ai-designer
The skills paradox found after the magic of AI image generation wains.
https://bootcamp.uxdesign.cc/the-skills-paradox-found-after-the-magic-of-ai-image-generation-wains-is-that-rather-than-6b91ba219b6d
The Turing Trap: The Promise & Peril of Human-Like Artificial Intelligence
https://digitaleconomy.stanford.edu/news/the-turing-trap-the-promise-peril-of-human-like-artificial-intelligence/
Introductions to prompts
Prompting is essentially
writing a design brief for an expert collaborator
. A good prompt gives the model the same grounding you'd give a teammate: context, goal, constraints, and examples. These links arguably some of the best places to go and get familiarized with prompting and some of the intricacies involved in it.
Google, Prompting Guide 101
https://services.google.com/fh/files/misc/gemini-for-google-workspace-prompting-guide-101.pdf
Anthropic, Prompt Engineering Overview
https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview
Anthropic, Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial
https://github.com/anthropics/prompt-eng-interactive-tutorial?tab=readme-ov-file
After completing this course, you will be able to: Understand the structure of a great prompt, Recognise common failure modes, Learn the 80/20 techniques to address them, Understand LLMs strengths and weaknesses, Build a strong prompts library
🗣️ Prompting
Prompting in itself is an evolving space and affected by new model types as they come out. The fundamentals seem reasonably set. The clearer you are and the more context you give, the better the outcomes can be. Most useful techniques sit on a continuum from lightweight chat to embedded automation.
A prompting maturity ladder
Ad-hoc chat
— one-off questions and quick experiments.
Reusable pattern
— save effective prompts as templates for repeat tasks.
Prompt library
— versioned collection shared across the team.
Prototyping scripts
— prompts wired into tools like Figma or the CLI.
Retrieval + tools
— model accesses design systems, research, or a11y checkers.
System prompts & agents
— embedded background instructions and automated reviewers.
Common approaches
Structured prompts
Use a short framework such as
CARE
(Context, Ask, Rules, Examples) or
WIRE+FRAME
(Who, Input, Rules, Expected … Evaluate). They keep outputs focused and repeatable.
CARE →
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/careful-prompts/
WIRE+FRAME →
https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2025/08/prompting-design-act-brief-guide-iterate-ai/
Stepwise reasoning
Ask the model to think or act in stages: diagnose → propose → check. This helps with complex reasoning like usability reviews or content checks.
Further reading
IBM:
What is chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting?
https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/chain-of-thoughts
Google Research:
Language models perform reasoning via chain-of-thought.
https://research.google/blog/language-models-perform-reasoning-via-chain-of-thought/
DataCamp:
Prompt chaining tutorial.
https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/prompt-chaining-llm
Personas and examples
Assign the AI a role ("accessibility auditor", "UX writer") and show two or three good examples. Roles anchor tone and accuracy; examples teach style.
Further reading
Learn Prompting:
Role prompting.
https://learnprompting.org/docs/advanced/zero_shot/role_prompting
arXiv:
When “A Helpful Assistant” Is Not Really Helpful: Personas in System Prompts Do Not Improve Performance.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.10054
Constraints and guardrails
List short "do" and "don't" points to prevent errors. For example:
Do
keep within 40 characters;
Don't
use technical jargon.
Further reading
ImageNinja blog:
7 best practices for prompt engineering to master in 2025.
https://blog.imageninja.ai/best-practices-for-prompt-engineering
Skywork AI:
Prompt engineering for Manus 1.5 — structure, guardrails, and evaluation.
https://skywork.ai/blog/ai-agent/prompt-engineering-manus-1-5-structure-guardrails-evaluation/
Critique-refine loop
Let the AI review its own draft against simple criteria ("clarity", "tone", "accuracy"), then improve it. One or two cycles are usually enough.
Grounding and retrieval
Paste in small extracts from your design system, research, or WCAG so the AI works with trusted information. Avoid long documents; summarise or link.
Further reading
Medium:
The architect’s guide to LLM system design: from prompt to production.
https://medium.com/%40vi.ha.engr/the-architects-guide-to-llm-system-design-from-prompt-to-production-8be21ebac8bc
Wikipedia:
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retrieval-augmented_generation
Team reuse
Save well-performing prompts in a small library. Note the task, date, and version. Over time these become your in-house "prompt patterns".
Further reading
Kinde:
Prompt patterns that scale — reusable LLM prompts for dev teams.
https://kinde.com/learn/ai-for-software-engineering/prompting/prompt-patterns-that-scale-reusable-llm-prompts-for-dev-eams/
White et al:
A prompt pattern catalog to enhance prompt engineering.
https://www.dre.vanderbilt.edu/~schmidt/PDF/prompt-patterns.pdf
When to use which
Goal
Reliable pattern
Confidence
Generate new ideas fast
Structured + persona + examples
High
Critique or QA a design
Role + stepwise heuristic check
Peer-reviewed
Write on-brand UI copy
Structured + constraints + examples
Field-tested
Prototype with context
Retrieval of design-system snippets
Medium
Accessibility & code audit
Role + tools (e.g. Axe + LLM)
High
Evidence is strongest for structured and role-based prompting, heuristic evaluations, and accessibility repair tasks; more experimental for multi-agent panels or fully automated prototyping.
Risks and checks
Bias and inclusion.
Ask explicitly for inclusive language; check diversity in examples.
Hallucination control.
Require citations or clear grounding; never accept unverified facts.
Privacy and IP.
Strip client or user data; prefer local models for confidential work.
Quality assurance.
Use your usual heuristics and rubrics before anything goes live.

🤷 Designing AI Experiences For Users

Multimodal AI Design Patterns
Multimodal AI systems can process and respond to multiple input/output modes simultaneously, combining text, voice, vision, audio, and video. This enables more natural, accessible, and context-aware interactions. When to use multimodal: when you need to reduce cognitive load (voice input while hands are busy), increase accessibility (multiple input methods), or provide richer context (vision + text together). Design considerations include modality selection (when to use voice vs. text vs. visual), input fusion (combining multiple inputs), and graceful degradation (fallback when one modality fails).
Design Patterns: Multimodal interaction patterns often involve context-aware modality selection. Use voice for hands-free tasks, visual for spatial understanding, text for precision. See existing pattern libraries above for multimodal examples.
Voice Interaction Design: When integrating voice, consider feedback patterns (how users know the system is listening), error recovery (what happens when speech isn't understood), and privacy (visual indicators for when audio is being processed).
Cross-modal Patterns: Combine modalities for richer experiences: visual search + voice query, image analysis + text description, video understanding + text transcription.
Accessibility Considerations: Multimodal design increases accessibility by offering alternative input methods. Ensure all core functionality works with each modality independently.
AI Agents & Agentic UX
Agentic UX refers to interfaces where AI agents act autonomously on behalf of users, making decisions, taking actions, and adapting over time. Unlike simple chatbots or assistants, agentic interfaces are proactive, goal-oriented, and capable of complex multi-step tasks. Key design considerations include: user control (how to interrupt, override, or steer agent behavior), transparency (making agent thinking and decisions visible), and trust (designing systems that are understandable and helpful). This becomes especially important when agents operate across multiple tools or have persistent memory.
Agent Management Interface Patterns, by Luke Wroblewski
https://lukew.com/ff/entry.asp?2106
- Patterns for managing and interacting with AI agents
Related: See
Designing Contextual Affordance
- Context management techniques relevant to agent design, including system prompts, memory, and tool selection
User Control and Override Patterns: Design clear ways for users to interrupt agent actions, adjust autonomy levels, and provide feedback. Consider visual indicators for agent state (thinking, acting, waiting for input).
Agent Transparency Patterns: Show what the agent is doing and why. Expose decision paths, tool usage, and reasoning steps. Balance transparency with cognitive load.
Designing For AI Engineers, by Eve Weinberg
https://uxdesign.cc/designing-for-ai-engineers-what-ui-patterns-and-principles-you-need-to-know-8b16a5b62a61
- UI patterns and principles for agent interfaces

🦾 Using AI To Get More Design Done
Empathy and user understanding
Enhancing Research Synthesis with AI: An Experiential Report
https://bootcamp.uxdesign.cc/enhancing-research-synthesis-with-ai-an-experiential-report-8341b2e3cc88
Problem framing
AI can enhance traditional problem framing by offering multiple perspectives, challenging assumptions, and helping you see problems systemically. Use AI as a thought partner to explore root causes, stakeholder alignment, and unintended consequences.
Problem-framing prompts for design and product thinking
HERE
- Structured prompts that provoke deeper, more critical responses from LLMs
AI Prompt Framing, by Raluca Budiu, Feifei Liu, Amy Zhang, Emma Cionca
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ai-prompt-structure/
- Research on structuring prompts for better AI problem framing
Using ai as thought partner:
https://open.substack.com/pub/onlydeadfish/p/fish-food-651-using-ai-as-a-thought?r=qj6x&utm_medium=ios
- Practical approach to using AI for structured thinking
Tip
Prompt that will guide you through creation of a product requirements document using an LLM
HERE
Creative ideation and refinement
AI excels at generating variations and unexpected connections, making it a powerful tool for ideation. Unlike traditional ideation, AI's variability becomes an asset. Each generation can reveal serendipitous features and alternative approaches you might not have considered. This is particularly valuable for the "matchmaking" approach: matching AI capabilities (even at moderate performance) with user needs to find low-effort, high-impact, buildable ideas.
AI Brainstorming Design Kit, by HCI Institute
https://aidesignkit.github.io/
- Framework that distills AI capabilities into eight core functions (Estimate, Forecast, Compare, Detect, Identify, Discover, Generate, Act). Offers a matchmaking approach to ideation that starts with AI capabilities rather than complex user pain points, leading to more buildable concepts.
Watch the intro on Youtube
.
Related: See
AI prototype mining
- A systematic approach to leveraging AI's variability for ideation through multiple prototype generation

Related Sub Pages
AI-assisted coding for creative prototyping
Iterative testing and feedback
Testing AI experiences requires different approaches than traditional UX testing. You're evaluating how well the system understands context, handles ambiguity, and recovers from errors, not just interface usability. Focus on testing prompt effectiveness, model behavior variations, and the latent interface (system prompts, memory, tools) as much as the visible UI.
Prompt Testing Frameworks: Test different prompt structures to see how AI interpretations vary. See
Problem Framing
section for structured prompt techniques.
User Testing AI Interfaces: What's Different? AI responses vary, so testing requires multiple iterations and focusing on edge cases, ambiguity handling, and error recovery.
Hallucination Detection: Watch for AI confidently presenting false information. Design for verification and correction patterns. See
Designing Contextual Affordance
for context management techniques.
AI Evaluation Frameworks: Traditional usability metrics need adaptation for AI. Consider accuracy, relevance, and user control alongside task completion.
AI UX Patterns for Trust:
https://catalogue.projectsbyif.com/
- Patterns for building trustworthy AI experiences
Collaboration and communication
Cognitive Challenges in Prompt Based Interactions with LLMs
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.14459
- Research on the cognitive load and communication challenges when working with LLMs
Storytelling and presentation
AI can accelerate presentation creation and help communicate complex AI concepts to stakeholders. Use AI to generate presentation content, but remember: the story and structure remain human-driven. When presenting AI features, focus on explaining capabilities, limitations, and user benefits rather than technical details.
Using AI for presentation design: Leverage AI tools to generate slides, visuals, and content outlines, but craft the narrative and flow yourself
Communicating AI concepts: Focus on user benefits, explain capabilities clearly, and be transparent about limitations. See
Mindset Change
for framing AI as infrastructure.
Storytelling frameworks for AI products: Explain what AI enables, not just what it does. Connect features to user outcomes and business value.

Cost & Token Management
Understanding token usage and costs is crucial when working with AI models, especially for prototyping and production workflows. Tokens are the basic units of text that models process. Both input and output consume tokens. Costs scale with token usage, so optimizing prompts and managing context length directly impacts expenses.
Decision Framework:
Cloud vs. Local:
Use cloud models for rapid prototyping, experimentation, and when you need the latest models. Use local models for data-sensitive work, cost control at scale, or when you need predictable costs.
Context Length:
Longer context windows (e.g., Claude 3.5 Sonnet's 200K tokens) enable richer prompts but cost more. Use shorter context for simple tasks.
Model Selection:
Different models have different pricing. Some optimize for speed (cheaper) vs. quality (more expensive). Match model capability to task complexity.
Token Efficiency:
Reuse system prompts, batch requests when possible, and structure prompts to minimize repetition. See
Prompt writing
section for optimization techniques.
Resources:
Token Counting Tools: Use online token counters or API tools to estimate costs before running prompts
Cost Calculators: Most providers offer pricing calculators on their websites. Estimate costs based on input/output token counts
Monitoring Usage: Set up usage alerts and track token consumption patterns to identify optimization opportunities
Local Model Considerations: Local models (LLaMA, Deepseek) eliminate per-token costs but require hardware resources. Useful for high-volume use cases or sensitive data.
References
A bunch of the links found here are from a posts by Vitaly Friedman 🙏
